item.title: The Starry Night
item.museum: The Museum of Modern Art, New York
item.lesson: Van Gogh painted his most famous canvas in June 1889 from his room in the asylum of Saint-Paul-de-Mausole in Saint-Rémy, where he had committed himself after the breakdown in Arles. The view through his barred window supplied the cypress and the hills; the village with its needle spire is part invention, more Dutch than Provençal; and the sky — eleven stars, a horned moon, and that colossal double spiral — came from somewhere no window faces. This was a departure he distrusted: van Gogh believed in painting from nature, and fretted in letters that working from imagination led astray; he called this one, almost dismissively, a 'study by night.' Astronomers note Venus really did blaze over Provence that month. The painting's cosmic turbulence has invited every reading from ecstasy to anguish; what is certain is the paint itself — ridged, directional, alive, every stroke a visible decision. Sold, traded, and finally acquired by MoMA in 1941, it became modern art's best-loved image: the night sky as a self-portrait.
item.lookFor: The cypress and the church spire are the painting's two verticals — one flame-dark and reaching, one small and man-made. Van Gogh, a former lay preacher, put the tree closer to heaven.
